required.title: [Read Aloud] The Story of the World, Volume 2: The Middle Ages
required.author: Susan Wise Bauer

required.title: [For Mentor] The History of the Medieval World
required.author: Susan Wise Bauer

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] The Door in the Wall
suggested.author: Marguerite de Angeli

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] D'Aulaires' Book of Norse Myths
[Read Aloud] Selections from One Thousand and One Nights

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] Genghis Khan and the Making of the Modern World
supplementary.author: Jack Weatherford

supplementary.title: [Read Aloud] The Saga of Erik the Viking
supplementary.author: Terry Jones

competence_markers.description: The student can narrate the basic story of what happened after Rome fell — the three forces (Church, Germanic kingdoms, Byzantium) that filled the vacuum
The student can describe Constantinople and explain why it was important
The student can narrate the rise of Islam and identify at least two achievements of Islamic civilization
The student can describe the feudal system in basic terms — who owed what to whom
The student can narrate the Viking story as more than raiding — including exploration, trade, and kingdom-building
The student can describe the Mongol Empire's scale and at least one surprising fact about it beyond destruction
The student is aware of Tang/Song China, West Africa (Mali/Timbuktu), and Japan as sophisticated civilizations contemporary with medieval Europe
The student can place major medieval events in rough chronological order and has a felt sense of the period's length (~1000 years)
The student engages with narration prompts with detail and personal interpretation, not rote recitation

narration_prompts.detail: What made it special? Where was it? What did it look like? Why was it important?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me the story of how Islam began and spread.
narration_prompts.detail: Who was Muhammad? What did he teach? How did Islam spread so quickly? What did the Islamic world build?
Were they just raiders? Where did they go? What did they build? What surprises you most about them?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about the Mongol Empire.
narration_prompts.detail: How large was it? How did the Mongols build it? What was terrifying about them? What was surprising about them?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about one civilization outside Europe during this period — Tang China, or the Mali Empire, or Japan.
narration_prompts.detail: What did they achieve? How does it compare to what was happening in Europe at the same time?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about the Black Death.
narration_prompts.detail: What was it? What happened? What changed because of it?

narration_prompts.prompt: Why do you think people built cathedrals that took longer than a lifetime to finish?
narration_prompts.detail: What did they believe that made them willing to start something they would never see completed?

cross_connections.description: The geography of the medieval world — Constantinople's strategic location, the Silk Road's route through Central Asia, the Viking sea routes, the Saharan trade routes — reinforces the geographic literacy developed in 1.2.1.

Christianity, Islam, Buddhism, and Norse mythology as worldviews that shaped entire civilizations. 1.1.3 introduces them as historical forces; 1.3.1 develops the framework for understanding why people believe what they believe.

Feudalism, the caliphate, the Mongol legal code (Yasa), and the Byzantine imperial system as different answers to the same question: how do you organize a large group of people? This connects directly to 1.4.1's exploration of governance.
Medieval building — castles, cathedrals, Viking longhouses — connects to the practical construction skills in 2.4.1.
The narrations that assess this packet build the same skills developed in 3.2.1. The rich stories provide material for increasingly detailed written narration.
Samurai Bushido, Viking honor, medieval chivalry, and Islamic codes of conduct — all are virtue systems worth comparing to the student's own developing character.

Byzantine mosaics, Islamic geometric art, Gothic cathedrals, Chinese porcelain, Japanese ink painting, Viking woodcarving — the aesthetic achievements introduced here are developed analytically in 3.4.7.

content.why_this_matters:
The ancient empires you studied in 1.1.1 and 1.1.2 did not simply vanish. They transformed. Rome fell in the West — but its eastern half kept going for another thousand years, speaking Greek and calling itself Roman. New religions — Christianity and Islam — reshaped entire civilizations. New peoples — the Vikings, the Mongols, the Franks — built kingdoms where empires had crumbled. And on the other side of the world, China reached heights of art and invention that Europe would not match for centuries.

The period you are about to study is sometimes called the "Middle Ages" or the "medieval period." Some people call it the Dark Ages, but that name is wrong. It was not dark. It was one of the most creative, violent, beautiful, and consequential periods in human history. Cathedrals that still stand were built during this period. The world's first universities were founded. The compass, gunpowder, and the printing press were invented. Scholars in Baghdad preserved the knowledge of ancient Greece when Europe had nearly lost it. Vikings sailed to North America five hundred years before Columbus. Mongol horsemen conquered the largest land empire in history.

This is the story of a world held together — and sometimes torn apart — by faith, by empire, and by the courage and cruelty of remarkable people.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **A vivid sense of the medieval world as real** — not a dusty chapter but a living world of castles, monasteries, ships, markets, deserts, and cathedrals populated by people as real as anyone alive today
2. **Understanding of Christianity and Islam as civilizational forces** — not just beliefs but systems that organized entire societies, built empires, created art, preserved knowledge, and motivated both extraordinary generosity and extraordinary violence
3. **Awareness of a genuinely global medieval world** — Europe, Byzantium, the Islamic world, Tang and Song China, the Vikings, the Mongols, Japan, West Africa, and the Americas were all developing simultaneously, often connected by trade
4. **The Vikings as more than raiders** — traders, explorers, settlers, and founders of kingdoms from Russia to Normandy to Iceland to North America
5. **The Mongol Empire as the largest land empire in history** — its scale, its violence, and its paradoxical consequences for trade and connection
6. **Basic timeline orientation** — the medieval period lasted roughly a thousand years (500-1500 CE), and the student should develop a felt sense of where major events sit within that span
content.core_content:
### Part 1: When Rome Fell — And What Came Next

You learned in 1.1.2 that the Western Roman Empire collapsed. By 476 CE, the last Roman emperor in the West was removed by a Germanic chieftain. Roads crumbled. Aqueducts broke. Trade networks that had connected Britain to Egypt fell apart. Cities shrank. In many places, people forgot how to read and write.

But this was not the end of everything. It was the end of one way of organizing civilization — and the beginning of new experiments.

Three great forces stepped into the vacuum left by Rome's fall:

**The Christian Church.** When the Roman government collapsed, the Church remained. Bishops became the most important leaders in many towns. Monasteries — communities of monks who had devoted their lives to God — became the places where books were copied, knowledge was preserved, and the poor were fed. Without the monasteries, much of what the ancient world knew would have been lost forever.

**The Germanic kingdoms.** The peoples who had migrated into Roman territory — Franks, Goths, Vandals, Angles, Saxons — did not destroy everything. They settled. They adopted some Roman customs, married Roman families, and eventually converted to Christianity. Over time, they built new kingdoms on the ruins of the old empire. The most successful of these was the Frankish kingdom, which would eventually become France and Germany.

**The Eastern Roman Empire — Byzantium.** Rome fell in the West, but not in the East. The eastern half of the Roman Empire, centered on the great city of Constantinople, continued for another thousand years. They called themselves Romans. They spoke Greek. They preserved Roman law, Greek philosophy, and Christian theology — and created something new from the combination.

### Part 2: Constantinople — The City Between Two Worlds

Constantinople was the greatest city in the medieval world for most of this period. Built on the narrow strait where Europe meets Asia, it sat at the crossroads of every major trade route — the Silk Road from China, the spice routes from India, the grain ships from Egypt, the furs and amber from Russia.

The city was magnificent. Its walls were the strongest in the world — a triple line of fortifications that kept invaders out for a thousand years. The Hagia Sophia — the Church of Holy Wisdom, built by Emperor Justinian in 537 CE — was the largest cathedral in the world for nearly a thousand years. Its massive dome seemed to float on light, and visitors who entered it for the first time often wept at its beauty.

Constantinople was also a city of learning. While Western Europe was struggling to preserve basic literacy, Constantinople had libraries, schools, and scholars who could read the ancient Greek philosophers in the original language. They preserved texts that the West had lost — works by Plato, Aristotle, Archimedes, and Galen. Without Constantinople, much of what we call "Western civilization" would have been forgotten entirely.

The Byzantine Empire was not just a museum of Roman and Greek culture. It was a living civilization that produced its own remarkable achievements: sophisticated law codes, brilliant art (the mosaics of Ravenna and Constantinople are among the most beautiful works of art ever created), complex diplomacy, and a form of Christianity — Eastern Orthodoxy — that shaped the civilizations of Russia, Greece, Serbia, Bulgaria, and Romania down to the present day.

### Part 3: The Rise of Islam — A New Faith Reshapes the World

In 610 CE, in the Arabian city of Mecca, a merchant named Muhammad received what he believed was a revelation from God — the same God worshipped by Jews and Christians. The angel Gabriel, Muhammad taught, brought him the words of God, which were recorded in the Quran. Muhammad preached that there was one God (Allah, the Arabic word for God), that all people were equal before God, and that the wealthy had a duty to care for the poor.

Not everyone in Mecca welcomed this message. The wealthy merchants who profited from Mecca's pagan shrine, the Kaaba, saw Muhammad as a threat. In 622 CE, Muhammad and his followers left Mecca for the city of Medina — a journey called the Hijra that marks the beginning of the Islamic calendar. In Medina, Muhammad became not just a religious teacher but a political and military leader. Within a decade, he had united most of Arabia under Islam.

After Muhammad's death in 632 CE, his followers — the caliphs — carried Islam outward with astonishing speed. Within a hundred years, the Islamic empire stretched from Spain to the borders of India. It was one of the fastest expansions in human history.

**What the Islamic world built:** The caliphs did not just conquer. They built. Baghdad, founded in 762 CE, became the largest and most sophisticated city in the world. The House of Wisdom in Baghdad gathered scholars — Muslim, Christian, Jewish, Persian, Indian — to translate the great works of Greek philosophy, Indian mathematics, and Persian astronomy into Arabic. Muslim scholars did not merely preserve this knowledge. They advanced it. They developed algebra (the word itself comes from Arabic — *al-jabr*). They made breakthroughs in optics, medicine, astronomy, and chemistry. They built hospitals, observatories, and universities centuries before Europe had them.

### Part 4: Medieval Europe — Castles, Cathedrals, and the Feudal Order

While Constantinople glittered and Baghdad compiled the world's knowledge, Western Europe was rebuilding from the ground up. The system that emerged was feudalism — a way of organizing society when there was no strong central government.

**How feudalism worked:** The king owned all the land — in theory. In practice, he granted large estates to powerful lords in exchange for military service. Those lords granted smaller pieces to lesser lords, who granted even smaller pieces to knights. At the bottom were the peasants — the people who actually worked the land. A peasant was often a serf, which meant they were bound to the land: they could not leave without their lord's permission. They owed their lord labor, a share of their crops, and obedience. In return, the lord owed them protection.

This was not slavery — serfs had rights, could own some property, and could not be bought or sold separately from the land. But it was not freedom as you understand it. Most people in medieval Europe were born on a piece of land, worked that land their entire lives, and died within a few miles of where they were born.

**The Church was everywhere.** The Catholic Church was the one institution that connected all of Western Europe. The Pope in Rome claimed spiritual authority over every Christian. Monasteries dotted the landscape. Parish churches stood in every village. The Church ran the only schools. Church courts handled disputes. Church holidays structured the year. For most medieval Europeans, the Church was not just part of life — it was the framework that gave life meaning.

**Monasteries as civilizational lifeboats.** When you think of a monk, you might imagine a quiet man praying. Medieval monks did pray — but they also farmed, brewed beer, made cheese, ran hospitals, taught children, and — most importantly for civilization — copied books. Before the printing press, every book had to be copied by hand. Monks spent years copying the Bible, the writings of the Church Fathers, and — crucially — the works of ancient Roman and Greek authors. Without the monks of medieval Europe, many of the texts that survived from the ancient world would have been lost.

### Part 5: The Vikings — Explorers, Traders, and Founders of Kingdoms

In the late 700s CE, ships appeared on the coasts of England and France — long, narrow ships with dragon-headed prows, carrying warriors who struck without warning and vanished before anyone could organize a defense. The Vikings had arrived.

The popular image of Vikings is of savage raiders. They were raiders — the attack on the monastery of Lindisfarne in 793 CE shocked all of Christendom. But they were far more than that.

**Viking explorers.** The Vikings were the greatest sailors of the medieval world. They navigated open ocean in ships built with extraordinary skill — the planks clinker-built, overlapping and riveted, creating vessels that were both strong and flexible. They settled Iceland (874 CE), colonized Greenland (986 CE), and reached North America around 1000 CE — nearly five hundred years before Columbus. At a place called L'Anse aux Meadows in Newfoundland, archaeologists have found the remains of a Viking settlement. This is not legend. It is proven archaeological fact.

**Viking traders.** Vikings did not just take things by force. They built one of the most extensive trade networks in the medieval world. Viking merchants traveled south through the rivers of Russia, reaching Constantinople and Baghdad. They traded furs, amber, and slaves for silk, silver, and spices. Swedish Vikings — called Varangians — founded the city of Novgorod and established the political entity that would eventually become Russia. The very word "Russia" may come from "Rus," the name given to these Swedish Vikings by the peoples they encountered.

**Viking kingdom-builders.** Vikings settled Normandy in northern France (the name means "land of the Northmen"). Their descendants — the Normans — conquered England in 1066 under William the Conqueror. Other Vikings ruled parts of Ireland, Scotland, and the islands between. They established kingdoms in Sicily and southern Italy. Vikings who had once raided monasteries eventually built churches of their own.

**Norse mythology.** The Vikings had a rich and dramatic mythology. Odin, the All-Father, gave up one of his eyes for wisdom and hung on the world-tree Yggdrasil for nine days to learn the secret of the runes. Thor wielded his hammer Mjolnir against the giants who threatened the world. Warriors who died bravely in battle went to Valhalla, the hall of the slain, to feast and fight until the end of the world — Ragnarok, the twilight of the gods. These were not simple fairy tales. They were the spiritual framework of a civilization that valued courage, cunning, and endurance above all things.

### Part 6: The Crusades — When Faiths Collided

In 1095 CE, Pope Urban II called on the Christians of Europe to march east and recapture the Holy Land — Jerusalem and the surrounding region — from Muslim control. Thousands answered. They sewed crosses onto their clothing (the word "crusade" comes from the Latin word for cross) and marched toward Jerusalem.

**Why it happened:** Jerusalem was sacred to Christians, Muslims, and Jews. It had been under Muslim control since 638 CE — more than four hundred years. For most of that time, Christian pilgrims had been allowed to visit. But political changes in the region made pilgrimage more dangerous, and the Byzantine emperor asked the Pope for military help against advancing Turkish armies.

**What happened:** The First Crusade (1096-1099) succeeded in capturing Jerusalem. The Crusaders established small kingdoms in the Holy Land. But the cost was terrible. On their way east, Crusaders massacred Jewish communities in the Rhineland — people who had nothing to do with the Holy Land but who were convenient targets for religious violence. When the Crusaders took Jerusalem, they killed Muslims, Jews, and even some Eastern Christians in the city.

The Crusades continued for nearly two hundred years. Muslims eventually recaptured Jerusalem under the brilliant commander Saladin in 1187 — and Saladin, notably, treated the defeated Christians with mercy and respect, in sharp contrast to the Crusaders' behavior when they had taken the city.

**What the Crusades produced:** Not just war. The Crusaders encountered a Muslim civilization that was, in many ways, more sophisticated than their own. They discovered foods they had never tasted (sugar, lemons, rice, spices), fabrics they had never seen (silk, cotton), and ideas they had never encountered. Trade between Europe and the Islamic world expanded enormously. Knowledge flowed westward. The Crusades, paradoxically, helped wake Europe up.

### Part 7: The Mongol Empire — The Largest Land Empire in History

In the early 1200s, a man named Temujin united the warring tribes of the Mongolian steppe. He took the title Genghis Khan — "universal ruler." What followed was the most explosive military expansion in human history.

**The scale:** Within fifty years, the Mongol Empire stretched from Korea to Hungary, from Siberia to the Persian Gulf. It covered 24 million square kilometers — the largest contiguous land empire ever. No empire before or since has controlled as much connected territory.

**The methods:** The Mongols were extraordinary horsemen and archers, but their real advantage was organization and psychological warfare. Genghis Khan used terror deliberately. Cities that surrendered were spared. Cities that resisted were destroyed utterly — every person killed, the buildings leveled. Word spread. Many cities chose surrender. The death toll was staggering: an estimated 30 to 40 million people, possibly ten percent of the world's entire population at that time.

**The destruction of Baghdad (1258):** The Mongols under Genghis Khan's grandson Hulagu destroyed Baghdad — the greatest city in the Islamic world. The last Abbasid caliph was killed. Libraries were burned. The House of Wisdom, which had preserved and advanced human knowledge for five hundred years, was destroyed. Books were reportedly thrown into the Tigris River in such quantities that the water ran black with ink.

**The paradox:** After the conquest came peace. The Pax Mongolica — the Mongol Peace — made travel across the entire breadth of Asia safe for the first time in history. A merchant could travel from Beijing to Baghdad without being robbed. The Silk Road hummed with trade. Ideas, technologies, and diseases flowed freely across the empire. Marco Polo made his famous journey to China during this period.

**What was unusual about Genghis Khan:** He was illiterate. He was born into poverty and hardship. Yet he created a legal code (the Yasa) that governed the empire. He practiced religious tolerance — Christians, Muslims, Buddhists, and Taoists all worshipped freely under Mongol rule. He promoted people based on ability, not birth. He was simultaneously one of history's greatest destroyers and one of its most effective organizers.

### Part 8: Tang and Song China — The Most Advanced Civilization on Earth

While Europe was rebuilding from Rome's fall, China was experiencing one of its golden ages. The Tang Dynasty (618-907 CE) and the Song Dynasty (960-1279 CE) produced a civilization that was, by most measures, the most advanced on Earth.

**What they achieved:**
- **Paper and printing.** The Chinese invented paper centuries earlier and developed block printing by the 700s. The world's first printed book — the Diamond Sutra — was produced in China in 868 CE, nearly six hundred years before Gutenberg's Bible. Movable type was invented in China by Bi Sheng around 1040 CE.
- **Gunpowder.** Invented by Chinese alchemists searching for an elixir of immortality. First used in fireworks, then in weapons. Gunpowder would eventually transform warfare worldwide.
- **The compass.** Chinese sailors used magnetic compasses for navigation centuries before European sailors did. This technology made long-distance sea travel reliable.
- **The Grand Canal.** One of the largest engineering projects in human history — over a thousand miles of artificial waterway connecting northern and southern China, allowing grain and goods to move across the empire.
- **Porcelain, silk, and tea.** Chinese artisans produced goods of a quality that no other civilization could match. European nobles paid fortunes for Chinese porcelain and silk. The English word "china" for fine pottery comes from the country itself.

The Song Dynasty capital, Hangzhou, may have been the largest city in the world in the 1200s, with over a million residents — at a time when London had perhaps 50,000. It had restaurants, theaters, bookshops, public bathhouses, and a fire department. Marco Polo, visiting in the late 1200s, called it "the finest and noblest city in the world."

### Part 9: West Africa — Kingdoms of Gold and Learning

While Europe built cathedrals and the Mongols built empires, West Africa produced kingdoms of extraordinary wealth and sophistication that most Western curricula barely mention.

**The Kingdom of Ghana (circa 300-1200 CE):** Not the same as modern Ghana — the ancient kingdom was located in what is now Mauritania and Mali. Ghana controlled the trade in gold and salt across the Sahara. Arab travelers described the king of Ghana sitting on a throne adorned with gold, surrounded by horses wearing cloth of gold.

**The Mali Empire (circa 1235-1600 CE):** Founded by Sundiata Keita, whose story — the Sundiata Epic — is one of the great oral epics of world literature. Mali grew to control the gold and salt trade and became one of the wealthiest empires in the world.

**Mansa Musa's pilgrimage (1324 CE):** Mansa Musa, emperor of Mali, made a pilgrimage to Mecca that became legendary. He traveled with a caravan of 60,000 people and gave away so much gold in Cairo that he crashed the Egyptian economy for a decade. He was quite possibly the richest person who has ever lived. On his return, he built mosques, schools, and libraries throughout Mali.

**Timbuktu:** A city in the Mali Empire that became one of the great centers of learning in the medieval world. The University of Sankore in Timbuktu had tens of thousands of students and a collection of hundreds of thousands of manuscripts — on astronomy, mathematics, medicine, law, and religion. These manuscripts still exist, and scholars are still working to preserve and study them.

### Part 10: Japan — Warriors and Poets

On the other side of the world, Japan was developing a civilization unlike any other — one that combined extraordinary martial culture with extraordinary artistic refinement.

**The Heian Period (794-1185 CE):** The imperial court at Kyoto produced one of the most refined cultures in human history. Courtiers wrote poetry, practiced calligraphy, and judged each other by the beauty of their clothing and the elegance of their manners. Lady Murasaki Shikibu wrote *The Tale of Genji*, which many scholars consider the world's first novel — a complex, psychologically rich story of love and loss in the Japanese court, written around 1000 CE, when most of Europe's literature was about saints and battles.

**The rise of the samurai:** Outside the elegant court, Japan was increasingly violent. Powerful families fought for control of land and power. A warrior class emerged — the samurai. They served lords with absolute loyalty, fought with sword and bow, and lived by a code of honor called Bushido. A samurai who failed in his duty was expected to take his own life rather than live with dishonor.

**Zen Buddhism and the samurai:** Zen Buddhism, which emphasized meditation, discipline, and direct experience of reality, became deeply connected to samurai culture. The samurai trained not just their bodies but their minds. The arts of the tea ceremony, flower arranging, and ink painting — all influenced by Zen — flourished alongside the arts of war. Japan produced a civilization where the same person might write a haiku in the morning and draw a sword in the afternoon.

### Part 11: The Silk Road — The Thread That Connected the World

Running from China through Central Asia to the Mediterranean, the Silk Road was not a single road but a network of trade routes that connected the major civilizations of the medieval world.

**What traveled along it:** Silk, spices, porcelain, and tea moved westward from China. Gold, silver, glass, and wool moved eastward from Europe and the Middle East. But goods were not the most important cargo. Ideas traveled the Silk Road: Buddhism spread from India to China along it. Islam spread eastward along it. Technologies — paper, gunpowder, the compass — moved from China to the Islamic world and then to Europe.

**And disease.** The Black Death — the bubonic plague that killed roughly a third of Europe's population between 1347 and 1351 — almost certainly traveled the Silk Road from Central Asia to the Mediterranean, carried by fleas on rats in merchant ships. The same routes that connected civilizations also connected their diseases.

The medieval world was not a collection of isolated civilizations. It was a connected system — and what happened in one part of it could transform everything else.

### Part 12: The Black Death — When a Third of Europe Died

In 1347, merchant ships arrived in Sicily carrying a terrible cargo. Rats on the ships bore fleas infected with the bacterium that causes bubonic plague. Within five years, roughly one-third of Europe's entire population was dead. Some estimates suggest 25 million people in Europe alone, and possibly 75 to 200 million across Eurasia.

**What it looked like:** Victims developed painful swellings in their armpits and groin. Their skin turned black in places. Most died within days. Entire villages were wiped out. Bodies lay in streets because there was no one left to bury them. Priests died attending the sick. Doctors died treating patients.

**What it changed:** The Black Death transformed Europe. With so many dead, labor became scarce and valuable. Surviving peasants could demand higher wages. The rigid feudal system began to crack. The psychological impact was enormous — people who had believed God was in control struggled to understand why He would permit such suffering. Some blamed the Jews. Jewish communities often had lower death rates, probably because hygienic practices in Jewish law (ritual hand-washing, dietary restrictions, community quarantine) offered some protection against the plague. This was interpreted not as evidence of good hygiene but as evidence of well-poisoning. Thousands of Jews were massacred across Europe during the plague years — scapegoated for a disaster they had no part in causing.

The world that emerged from the Black Death was different from the one that had entered it. Europe was shaken, changed, and — eventually — ready for something new.
content.formative_practices:
*History at Stretching Branches is learned through reading, listening, looking, and talking. The following are guided studies that the student works through with their mentor over the course of this packet.*

### Study 1: The Read-Through [Ongoing — Backbone of the Packet]
Read the relevant chapters of *The Story of the World, Volume 2* aloud, in order. After each chapter or section, the student narrates back what they heard — in their own words, with as much detail as they can produce. Supplement with Eyewitness pages and map work after each reading.

### Study 2: Map of the Medieval World [Early in the Packet]
On a large map, locate and label: Constantinople, Baghdad, Rome, Paris, London, Mecca, Medina, Timbuktu, the Mongol Empire's extent, Viking routes (including the route to North America), the Silk Road, Tang China, Kyoto. This map stays on the wall throughout the packet. Add new locations as you encounter them.

### Study 3: Cathedral and Mosque [Mid-Packet]
Using David Macaulay's *Cathedral* and *Mosque* (or equivalent), study how these great buildings were constructed. Key discussion: both took decades or generations to build. What kind of society builds something that the builders themselves will never see finished? What does that tell you about what they believed?

### Study 4: Viking Exploration [Mid-Packet]
Trace Viking routes on the map — from Scandinavia to Iceland, Greenland, Newfoundland, through Russia to Constantinople, to Normandy, to Sicily. Key discussion: the Vikings reached North America around 1000 CE. Why does almost nobody talk about this? Why is Columbus the famous name instead?

### Study 5: The Mongol Empire [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Study the Mongol expansion on the map. Key questions: How large was the empire compared to any other you have studied? How did a relatively small group of people conquer so much? What happened after the conquest — was it only destruction, or did something come from it?

### Study 6: Tang China Investigation [Late Packet]
Study the inventions of Tang and Song China: paper, printing, gunpowder, the compass, porcelain. Key discussion: all of these were invented in China long before Europe had them. Why do most people in the West not know this? What does this tell you about whose story usually gets told?

### Study 7: West African Kingdoms [Late Packet]
Study the Mali Empire and Timbuktu. Key questions: Mansa Musa may have been the richest person who ever lived. Why have most people never heard of him? Timbuktu had a great university when many European cities had none. What does this change about how you picture the medieval world?

### Study 8: Timeline Construction [End of Packet]
Extend the timeline from 1.1.1 and 1.1.2. Mark: Fall of Rome (476 CE), founding of Islam (622 CE), Charlemagne crowned (800 CE), Viking settlement of Iceland (874 CE), Vikings in North America (~1000 CE), the First Crusade (1096), Genghis Khan's conquests (1206-1227), destruction of Baghdad (1258), Mansa Musa's pilgrimage (1324), the Black Death (1347-1351). Key observation: the medieval period lasted roughly a thousand years — as long as the entire Roman Empire.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Narration Quality
**Developing well:** The student retells medieval history with specific detail, personal interpretation, and a sense of sequence. Narrations include specific names (Genghis Khan, Mansa Musa, Muhammad, Saladin), specific places (Constantinople, Baghdad, Timbuktu), and specific achievements. The student asks questions and makes connections between civilizations.

**Developing slowly:** Narrations are vague and generic: "There were knights and castles." No specific names, places, or events. No questions or connections.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the reading material engaging the student? (Try different resources — some students respond better to visual materials like Macaulay's books than to narrative text.) Is the reading happening at a good time of day? Are narration prompts specific enough?

### 2. Global Awareness
**Developing well:** The student can talk about medieval civilizations beyond Europe with specific detail. They know that Baghdad had the House of Wisdom while Europe was rebuilding. They know Mansa Musa was phenomenally wealthy. They know Tang China was the most technologically advanced civilization on Earth. The medieval world in their imagination has multiple centers, not just one.

**Developing slowly:** The student defaults to "medieval = Europe." Non-European civilizations do not register or are quickly forgotten.

**Diagnostic questions:** Did you give non-European civilizations equal reading time and discussion time? If the student has only read about European medieval life, the imbalance is in the coverage, not the student.

### 3. Timeline Sense
**Developing well:** The student has a felt sense that the medieval period lasted roughly a thousand years and can place major events in rough order. They understand that Tang China and medieval Europe were happening at the same time.

**Developing slowly:** Everything medieval feels like one undifferentiated lump. The student cannot distinguish early medieval from late medieval or place events in relation to each other.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student built or interacted with a physical timeline? The timeline project at the end of the packet is essential, not optional.

### 4. Understanding of Faith as Civilizational Force
**Developing well:** The student understands that Christianity and Islam were not just things people believed but systems that organized entire civilizations — building universities, preserving knowledge, motivating conquest, shaping daily life. They can describe what monasteries did, what the House of Wisdom was, why cathedrals and mosques were built.

**Developing slowly:** Religion in the medieval world is vague or abstract. The student knows people were religious but cannot describe what that meant in practice.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you spent enough time on the concrete expressions of faith — buildings, institutions, daily practices? The Macaulay books are excellent for making faith concrete through architecture.

### 5. The Scapegoating Pattern
**Developing well:** The student understands that the Jewish communities were blamed for the Black Death, that this was wrong, and that it happened because scared people look for someone to blame. They may connect this to other instances of unfairness they understand.

**Developing slowly:** The scapegoating pattern does not register or the student accepts it as normal. The student does not express moral reaction to the persecution of innocent people.

**Diagnostic questions:** Did you discuss the Black Death's impact on Jewish communities directly? If it was mentioned in passing during the read-through but never discussed, it may not have landed.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Europe Monopoly
The single most common failure mode for this packet. Medieval Europe is well-resourced in children's literature. Castles and knights are popular with young students. It is easy for Europe to swallow the entire packet. Guard against this actively. Schedule specific reading sessions for non-European civilizations. Give them narration prompts of their own.

### Turning the Crusades into a Simple Story
The temptation is to present the Crusades as either "brave Christians fighting for the Holy Land" or "violent Christians attacking peaceful Muslims." Neither is adequate. At Stretching Branches, the student needs to understand: there were real reasons people went on Crusade; terrible things were done during the Crusades; the encounter between civilizations had consequences beyond the fighting; both sides had brave and brutal people. You do not need nuanced historiography — you need honest storytelling.

### Skipping the Mongols' Complexity
Genghis Khan is easy to present as a pure villain — the death toll makes it tempting. But the student packet deliberately includes his religious tolerance, his promotion by merit, and the Pax Mongolica. The student should encounter complexity: a man who destroyed more than almost anyone in history but also created conditions for unprecedented connection and exchange. This is not moral relativism — it is honest history.

### Treating the Black Death as Just a Disease Story
The Black Death's significance is not just epidemiological — it is civilizational. It cracked feudalism, challenged religious authority, transformed the labor market, and prompted the scapegoating and massacre of Jews. All of these consequences matter. The disease is the dramatic story; the consequences are the educational payload.

### Rushing the Packet
A thousand years of global history cannot be covered in a few weeks. This packet should take months. Rushing through it produces a student with surface-level impressions rather than vivid, specific knowledge. Better to cover fewer civilizations deeply than all of them shallowly — but aim for all of them with real time.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Constantinople
**Strong:** Describes the city's location (where Europe meets Asia), its walls, the Hagia Sophia, its role as a center of learning and trade. May compare it favorably to Western European cities of the same period. Expresses a personal reaction — wonder, curiosity.
**Weak:** "It was a big city." No specific detail, no sense of why it mattered.

### Prompt 2: The Rise of Islam
**Strong:** Tells Muhammad's story with key details — the revelation, the Hijra, the rapid expansion after his death. Describes at least one achievement of Islamic civilization (the House of Wisdom, algebra, hospitals). Understands that Islam built as well as conquered.
**Weak:** "Muhammad started Islam and it spread." No detail about how or what was built.

### Prompt 3: The Vikings
**Strong:** Goes beyond raiding to describe exploration (Iceland, Greenland, North America), trade (through Russia to Constantinople), and kingdom-building (Normandy, England). May express surprise or excitement about the North American settlement.
**Weak:** "Vikings were raiders." The popular image unreconstructed.

### Prompt 4: The Mongol Empire
**Strong:** Conveys the scale (largest land empire ever), the methods (psychological warfare, destruction of resisting cities), and the paradox (destruction followed by peace and trade). Mentions at least one surprising quality of Genghis Khan (religious tolerance, meritocracy).
**Weak:** "They conquered everything and killed people." No complexity, no paradox.

### Prompt 5: Non-European Civilization
**Strong:** Specific detail about the chosen civilization — Chinese inventions, Mansa Musa's wealth, Japanese samurai culture. Compares to Europe: "China had printing six hundred years before Europe." Shows understanding that Europe was not the center of the medieval world.
**Weak:** Vague generalities. Cannot produce specific facts about any non-European medieval civilization.

### Prompt 6: The Black Death
**Strong:** Describes the plague, its scale (a third of Europe), and at least one consequence (labor scarcity, feudalism cracking, persecution of Jews). May express emotional reaction to the scale of death.
**Weak:** "Lots of people died from a disease." No consequences, no detail.

### Prompt 7: Cathedral Building
**Strong:** Engages with the question personally. Offers an interpretation — "They believed in something bigger than themselves" or "They wanted to build something beautiful for God even if they would never see it finished." Shows understanding that faith motivated extraordinary collective effort.
**Weak:** "They built big churches." No engagement with the why.
